sentences.text: I would like to come back to a story during the war in Iasi.
After the program, new rules and regulations, anti-Semitic, and in line with the Nazi policy, included the wearing of the yellow star for the whole population of Jewish blood.
The stars weren't supplied by the government.
And the only thing that they published was the standard dimension and the material to be made of.
Of course, this was to the task of the women in the families to produce these yellow stars.
I still can remember what they looked like.
It was a round patch, black background, and two triangles of yellow fabric sewn on top.
And to hold this in a straight position, it was a piece of cardboard, a round piece of cardboard, on which the black material was attached.
There were various patterns of this yellow star.
The only thing it was the dimension, it needed to be of a certain size, around the size of a [non-English speech].
Quite big, particularly for a boy of six.
The sizes were-- it was only one size for all.
And I felt very proud to have such a nice decoration on me.
Looks like I was on the front line and having the decorations similar to the Iron Cross.
And my mother excelled in producing very, very smart yellow stars for the whole family.
Actually, she made the black background out of velvet.
Very smart.
But this still didn't help.
They needed to be sewn every day on various shirts or jackets.
It was the instruction that they must be sewn, not to be taken and put in the pocket, and put again, and taken off.
They must be there, fixed properly.
And this was in the winter.
And my father built me a very smart fur hat, a caracole, fur hat.
And I was very proud of this.
And walking in the street outside of our house, there were big piles of snow in the street.
And I was playing, making snowballs there.
And all of a sudden came a group of older children, Christian.
They saw me playing there.
They saw my yellow star.
They pushed me in the snow.
They beat me up, and they stole my fur hat.
I was smashed.
Not as much from the beating, but from the fact that I lost my smart fur hat.
Yes.
The bar mitzvah took place in Bucharest.
The synagogue was called the Athena Synagogue, like Athena, the capital city of Greece, because the street where the synagogue was called Athena Street.
I prepared for this bar mitzvah.
I had a teacher for a year.
He taught me the Hebrew alphabet.
He taught me how to read from the Torah.
He taught a big speech for thanking my parents for what they did for me.
It took place in the morning.
And my parents organized, after the bar mitzvah, the proper bar mitzvah, organized snacks, and drinks, and cakes in the entrance hall of the shul.
I was taught how to wear a t-fillin.
I was given a tallit and t-fillin, which I put them for a month.
That was all.
And we did have a dinner dance afterwards.
That was all my bar mitzvah.
The whole family attended.
And what was interesting-- this was in 1948, when people were scared to have religious functions or religious affiliations.
But my father didn't have any doubt that I should have a bar mitzvah.
Others, who belonged to the Communist party, they didn't dare to bring their children to the Torah.
So I had a proper bar mitzvah.
Yes.
After I finished at the age of 17 the high school and had the metric.
I had to pass an examination.
It was an exam, an admission exam, to the Institute of Civil Engineering.
I passed in the first 10, this exam, out of 500 candidates.
And for five years, I learned civil engineering in parallel.

Because I had a lot of affinity with mathematics and mathematics was my hobby for many years before I started the University, I learned in parallel at the University of Bucharest the Faculty of Mathematics Applied Mechanics.
So practically, I graduated simultaneously in two fields.
Things went from bad to worse in Romania in those times.
The Communist Party was taking control over the private lives of everybody.
I was like most, the great majority of students.
I was a member of the Youth Communist Party section.
And they organized all kinds of meetings in the interest of the policies, which the Communist Party was promoting in Romania.
It was important for them that people will declare what was their parent's profession and political affiliation before the war, or immediately after the war.
And those youngsters whose parents were considered bourgeoisie, like my father, who was a shopkeeper, or had the private business, were excluded from universities.
They were not even allowed to be accepted for the examination.
I lied.
I declared that my father was an accountant that didn't have a shop.
Otherwise, I couldn't become a student.
Doesn't matter what marks you have or anything, you were excluded from the university on the grounds of social origin.
And like me, there were many others who hid their social origin, in order to be able to study.
In the last year of university, the Communist pressure became unbearable.
They started to check the validity of the curriculum vitae of all the students, to see if they told the truth or not.
And in our hearts, we were frightened that they may discover that some of us lied.
They didn't find that I lied.
They didn't have a proof.
But they found that one of our colleagues, through he was denounced by somebody else, by sort of a collaborator, a Communist collaborator, that his father owned cafeteria.
He declared that the father was an employee.
But actually, he was a partner.
It was like a bakery, producing cakes and this sort of things.
Immediately, a big meeting with the 200 students of our year was organized.
And denunciation speeches were given.
And we were asked to expel our colleague.
It was put for an open vote.
And with the exceptional of a few, nobody voted for this.
This was a slap in the face of the local Communist boss.
Immediately, they organized a better meeting where they took people and they started to blackmail, in order to vote.
And I was one of the top students in that year.
And they asked me to vote against this colleague of mine.
And if not-- this was just a couple of days before graduation.
Normally, the top students were taken as staff at the university.
And if I would not vote, not only that I would not get in the university as a lecturer or assistant, but I won't get a job any close to my town.
And the same blackmail went to most of the people.
They put it to vote, and there was very few exceptions.
People didn't vote.
I didn't vote.
I couldn't do this, in spite of being on their list.
The result was that they expelled this colleague of ours from university, when he was about to graduate.
So he didn't graduate.
All those years were lost for him.
And when I graduated, they send me to a country place.
It was like exile, about 700 kilometers away from Bucharest.
That's the punishment.
But I have to say that, except for this incident, I was treated as an equal in the university.
I never suffered from anti-Semitism or bad treatment.
And I was always friendly with most of my colleagues, except for those who were real hard core Communists, which, in many cases, were former Legionnaires who turned Communist overnight.
Yes.
It was a general rule.
I did my military service in the form which was organized for us students.
It was during the five years of studies, one day each every week, and one month every year.
The one day every week was in the form of studies at university, military studies.
I was in the engineer's corps.
And the month's training was a large distance from Bucharest.
And we spent a full month training in proper uniforms, armament, and so on.
Then I became an officer in the Romanian Army.
I didn't practice anything.
I just got the grade, and that was all.
And this was actually a result of my following action after graduation.
I graduated in 1957.
And I couldn't stand the Communist system, simply couldn't stand the Communist system.
But it was impossible to leave the country.
The whole country was a jail.
And we were behind the iron curtain, which was impossible to cross it, to go to the Western Europe.

So in the autumn of 1958, a year after graduation, while I was working on one of the construction sites in Bucharest, all of a sudden-- I can't explain what happened-- it was accepted that the Jews can apply for leaving the country to go to Israel.
So Romania had a large Jewish population, even after the war, after the program and other things which happened.
Apparently, before the war, the Romanian population exceeded half a million-- Jewish population exceeded half a million people.
After the war, probably, they were left 253,000 people.
And one of the main centers was Bucharest.
So after this rumor spread that Jews can apply for passports to go to Israel, long queues in the front of the main police station of Bucharest of Jews started to take place.
They were standing in the queue for day and night.
I, my father, and my mother, we standed on this queue for two days and a night to get the forms for application for us to immigrate to Israel.
And sometimes, when so many Jews were standing in a queue-- there were still this iron guard people alive around.
And I heard some comments from them.
Said, what a good job a machine gun would have done with this queue, having probably the memories of what happened at Iasi.
And we applied in 1958.
The immediate result was that I was declared enemy of the regime, together with my father, of course.
And I was thrown out from the youth organization of Communist Party, of course.
I had to return my membership card in a not so pleasant meeting where people spoke against my betrayal.
And of course, I wasn't considered eligible for the Army any more, in spite of being an officer.
And I was thrown out from my job, where only government jobs exist.
And I stayed unemployed for about six months.
The only thing that I could do was to learn bridge.
That was my activity.
Eventually, they released the tough regulations.
And I was allowed to get a job as a civil engineer in a place where nobody wanted to go, somewhere close to the border.
But not having any choice, I had to accept it.
And I went there, and I worked, but with a feeling of being under punishment.
I applied time and again to get a passport.
And it was impossible to get one, because they let out of the country, generally, people without professions, or very old, who were a burden for the country.
But young people, like me, and with a good profession, they wouldn't let out, by any means.
And I was paid a fraction of the salary of one who didn't apply for immigration.
And I was given the most menial engineering jobs possible.
But I knew that I did this on purpose.
I knew that I took the chance on my own, so I didn't complain about all this.
After a couple of years, in 1961, I managed to get a job, also not so well-paid, below the average in Bucharest, in a design office, government design office, where I worked until 1969.
And now, if people remember, in 1968, it happened in Czechoslovakia what was called The Springs of Prague, when the Dubcek took command and tried to liberalize Czechoslovakia.
Between Springs and August, the Russians who didn't accept this sort of liberalization prepared to stop this movement.
And in August, 1969, the Russian troops occupied Czechoslovakia.
We, in Romania, felt very insecure, because Romania took, at that time, the side of Czechoslovakia.
And we were scared that the Russians will do to Romania what they did to Czechoslovakia, a full occupation.
But it didn't happen.
So a year passed.
And then, I planned my escape from Romania.
There were already 12 years since I applied for my immigration with all the frustrations, and the demotions, and ill-treatment, and blackmail.
So I decided to take my life in my hands and to escape from this big jail.
